proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: 5% of the city's population voluntarily walking/biking to work on a single day, simultaneous government mandate for home-office, and the immediate development of a custom tracking app. The causal chain assumes that a one-day behavioral nudge will trigger complex systemic changes (e.g., 2% of bus lines converting to electric, 1% city demand covered by solar) without intermediate policy or infrastructure steps. The dependency on 'Google' to develop the app and 'Government' to re-route transport creates a high-risk implementation bottleneck.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada. The only novel element is the proposed aggregation of 'big data' via a smartphone app, but the technical approach (using existing footprint algorithms) is standard. The novelty is low because the intervention is a repetition of known awareness campaigns rather than a new mechanism for emission reduction.

impact.rationale: The quantitative claims are scientifically unsound due to massive extrapolation errors. The proposal calculates long-term impacts (e.g., 35,700 tonnes of CO2e saved from electric buses) as a direct result of a one-day walking event, ignoring the multi-year capital investment and policy cycles required to deploy electric buses. The extrapolation from Buenos Aires to the world (scaling 5% participation to 1 billion people) assumes linear scalability of behavioral change, which contradicts empirical evidence on diffusion of innovations. The short-term impact (66 tCO2e) is negligible on a global scale, and the long-term attribution is causally unproven.

presentation.rationale: The proposal lacks scientific rigor in its data presentation. It mixes short-term behavioral changes with long-term infrastructure outcomes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) without distinguishing between correlation and causation. The mathematical derivations contain unit inconsistencies and circular logic (e.g., assuming the event causes the approval of solar laws). The text relies heavily on 'we expect' and 'we forecast' without citing empirical baselines or sensitivity analyses for the variables used.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a one-day awareness event with long-term structural decarbonization. The causal mechanisms linking a single day of walking to the deployment of electric bus fleets and solar legislation are non-existent. The quantitative impact model is fundamentally flawed, treating complex, capital-intensive infrastructure transitions as immediate, linear consequences of a behavioral nudge. While the intent is positive, the scientific plausibility of the claimed outcomes is extremely low.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal attributes long-term infrastructure outcomes (electric bus deployment, solar law approval) to a single-day awareness event without explaining the intermediate policy or investment mechanisms.
Extrapolation Error: Scaling a 5% participation rate in Buenos Aires to a global population assumes uniform behavioral adoption, ignoring cultural, infrastructural, and economic heterogeneity.

Implementation Risk: The plan depends on the immediate, voluntary cooperation of Google for app development and the government for transport re-routing, which are high-friction, low-probability events.
Data Integrity: The 'big data' value proposition is speculative; without a validated baseline of individual commuting habits, the calculated GHG savings are likely inaccurate.

System Interdependencies: The proposal ignores the 'rebound effect' and the fact that public transport (buses/subways) is often more efficient per capita than walking for long distances; skipping them entirely may not yield the net savings claimed if the alternative is inefficient private transport or if the walking distance is negligible compared to total commute.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' governance assumption: that a single-day awareness event can force structural changes in public transport efficiency, pass distributed solar laws, and mandate corporate home-office policies. The text explicitly states the government 'will have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes' and 'ask for their cooperation by allowing employees to work from home,' ignoring the legal, labor, and operational inertia required to enact such changes. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily track data without a robust privacy framework or data governance strategy presents significant regulatory and logistical hurdles.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day) with no novel governance mechanisms or policy innovations proposed. The proposal attempts to rebrand existing initiatives (like Buenos Aires' 'Ecobici') as a new project without introducing a new institutional model or regulatory lever. The 'novelty' is purely rhetorical, not structural.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically unsound and politically naive. The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral shift with long-term structural policy outcomes (e.g., claiming a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of a solar law as direct results of a walking event). The extrapolation of 39 million tCO2e/year globally based on a single-day event in Buenos Aires is a gross overestimation that ignores the difference between a temporary anomaly and sustained behavioral change. The governance capacity to enforce the 'long-term' impacts listed (e.g., 2% electric bus implementation) is not demonstrated.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Canvas, Impact, Costs), but the internal logic is fractured. It oscillates between a simple awareness campaign and a complex policy reform agenda without explaining the causal link between the two. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the lack of a clear, step-by-step regulatory pathway for the government's role weaken the professional presentation. The tone is overly optimistic and lacks the rigor expected for a policy proposal.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance perspective, this proposal is a classic case of 'solutionism' where a simple behavioral nudge is expected to solve complex systemic policy failures. It fails to distinguish between an event (which can be organized) and a policy regime (which requires legislation, budget, and enforcement). The proposal assumes the government will act as a passive enabler rather than a complex stakeholder with competing priorities, rendering the long-term impact claims unrealistic without a detailed implementation roadmap.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes a causal link between a one-day walking event and the passage of complex legislation (Distributed Solar Law) and operational changes in public transport, which is institutionally incoherent.

There is no data governance strategy for the proposed smartphone app; collecting location and movement data from 5% of the population raises significant privacy and security concerns that are not addressed.

The 'long-term' impact metrics (e.g., 10% recycling increase) are presented as direct outcomes of the event without a mechanism for enforcement or sustained behavioral change, making the impact claims speculative and likely inflated.

The proposal relies on 'voluntary' corporate cooperation for home-office days, ignoring labor laws, union agreements, and operational constraints that would likely block such mandates without formal policy intervention.
The extrapolation of local, one-day data to global, annual emission savings is methodologically flawed and undermines the credibility of the impact assessment.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big government' coordination model that is operationally fragile: it assumes the government will simultaneously mandate home-office days, re-route public transport, and authorize a city-wide event without a clear implementation roadmap. The technical requirement for a custom app to track routes and calculate GHG savings in real-time, while expecting Google to build it for free, is a critical resource gap. Furthermore, the assumption that 5% of the population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily walk to work on a single day ignores the logistical reality of Buenos Aires' density and the lack of infrastructure for such a mass shift in a single day.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a well-established global practice (referenced correctly in the text as existing in the US/Canada). The proposal adds a 'gamification' layer via an app and data tracking, but this is a standard feature in existing fitness and mobility apps (e.g., Strava, Citymapper). There is no novel mechanism for behavior change or carbon accounting; it is a repackaging of existing awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically unsound and operationally unrealistic. The proposal extrapolates a one-day event's short-term savings (66 tCO2e) into long-term structural changes (e.g., 35,700 tonnes saved via electric buses) based on a 5% participation rate. This conflates a one-off awareness event with systemic infrastructure policy. The claim that a single day of walking will lead to a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws is a logical leap with no causal mechanism provided. The 'Big Data' value is speculative without a guaranteed user base or data privacy framework.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Model Canvas, Impact calculations), but the internal logic is inconsistent. It oscillates between a 'practice' and a 'project' without defining the operational scope clearly. The reliance on external entities (Google, Government, MIT) to execute critical path items (app development, policy changes) without a defined governance structure or budget makes the narrative optimistic rather than pragmatic. The inclusion of image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without the actual content in the text reduces clarity.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' masquerading as a scalable climate solution. It fails to account for the massive coordination costs, the lack of a sustainable business model for the app, and the logical fallacy of deriving long-term policy outcomes from a single-day behavioral nudge. The reliance on 'altruistic spirit' and free government/tech support is a high-risk assumption that ignores the resource intensity of city-wide logistics.
key_concerns: Operational Feasibility: How does the proposal secure government authorization to re-route public transport and mandate home-office days without a legislative or executive order? This is a political, not just logistical, hurdle.

Resource Gap: The proposal assumes Google will develop a custom tracking app for free. What is the fallback if this does not happen? Who funds the security, medical, and media costs listed in the 'Costs' section?

Causal Logic: The jump from a one-day walking event to a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of solar laws is unsupported. What is the specific mechanism that translates 'walking' into 'policy change'?
Data Privacy & Liability: Collecting location data for 145,000+ users on a single day raises significant privacy and liability issues (e.g., accidents, data breaches) that are not addressed.

Scalability: The model relies on high-density urban centers (Buenos Aires). How does this scale to lower-density areas or countries with different public transport infrastructures (e.g., US suburbs) where walking is not a viable commute option?

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will instantly re-route public transport and force companies to allow home office for a single-day event without a pilot phase. The requirement for a custom Google-developed app to track specific GHG savings in real-time is technically and bureaucratically unrealistic for a one-off awareness day. Furthermore, the logistics of providing health support and security for a city-wide walking event without a clear route management plan present significant safety risks.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Bike to Work' or 'Walk to Work' days—is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada and the local 'Ecobici' system. The only slight variation is the attempt to gamify it with a specific GHG-tracking app, but the underlying behavioral mechanism is identical to decades of existing campaigns.

impact.rationale: The impact calculations are dangerously inflated and disconnected from ground reality. Extrapolating a single day's savings to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally is mathematically unsound and ignores the rebound effect (people driving more the next day). The proposal assumes a 5% participation rate will trigger systemic changes like a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws, which are policy outcomes, not direct results of a one-day walk. The actual short-term impact is negligible compared to the massive long-term claims.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard business canvas elements and includes specific (albeit flawed) calculations, which shows effort. However, the presentation suffers from a lack of critical self-reflection regarding the 'unintended effects' on vulnerable populations. It presents an idealized, frictionless version of the city that ignores the reality of Buenos Aires' infrastructure gaps, safety concerns for pedestrians, and the accessibility needs of those who cannot walk or bike.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down exercise in 'feel-good' metrics that ignores the daily friction of living in a dense, unequal city. It assumes everyone has the physical ability to walk 15 blocks, the safety to do so, and the privilege to work from home. The massive extrapolation of impact suggests a disconnect between the planners and the actual lived experience of the community, where a single day of walking does not magically fix systemic transport or policy failures.
key_concerns: Accessibility Exclusion: The proposal assumes universal physical ability to walk or bike, ignoring the elderly, disabled, and those with chronic conditions who rely on public transport or cars for mobility.

Safety and Infrastructure: Buenos Aires has significant gaps in safe pedestrian and cycling infrastructure. Encouraging mass walking/biking without addressing traffic safety or sidewalk conditions could lead to accidents, particularly for children and the elderly.

The 'Home Office' Privilege: The suggestion that people far from work can simply 'do home office' ignores the reality of the gig economy, service sector workers, and low-income employees who cannot work remotely.

Data Privacy and Surveillance: The proposal relies on a smartphone app tracking location and movement data for 'Big Data' analysis by sponsors and Google, raising serious privacy concerns for participants without clear consent frameworks.

Unrealistic Policy Leverage: The assumption that a one-day event will force the government to re-route buses or pass solar laws is a fundamental misunderstanding of how policy change works; this risks cynicism if the promised systemic changes do not materialize.

Rebound Effect: There is no plan to ensure that the 'saved' emissions aren't simply offset by increased driving on other days or by the carbon footprint of the event's logistics (media, prizes, app development).

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will mandate home office and restructure bus routes based on a single day's event data. The text admits the government must 'arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes,' a logistical impossibility for a one-day awareness event. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and immediate government authorization ignores the bureaucratic inertia and data privacy hurdles inherent in such public-private data exchanges.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Bike to Work' or 'Walk to Work' days—is a well-established global practice, not an innovation. The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada and Buenos Aires' own 'Ecobici' system. The only novel element is the aggregation of data for 'big data' insights, but the methodology (tracking steps to calculate carbon savings) is standard and lacks a unique mechanism for behavioral change beyond existing campaigns.

impact.rationale: The proposal hides a severe distributional injustice and a logical fallacy in its impact claims. It assumes a 5% participation rate will trigger a 10% increase in recycling and a 2% adoption of electric buses over five years, a causal link that is entirely speculative. More critically, the proposal excludes low-income populations who cannot afford to 'skip the bus' or work from home; for them, public transport is a necessity, not a choice. The 'savings' calculated ignore the fact that the poor often live in disconnected areas where walking is unsafe or impossible, effectively penalizing the most vulnerable for not participating.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is structurally coherent but normatively naive. It uses a Business Model Canvas and specific calculations to lend scientific weight to what are essentially aspirational guesses. The text fails to address the 'who bears the cost' question, presenting the event as universally beneficial while ignoring the safety risks of walking in high-density, high-traffic areas without infrastructure upgrades, and the economic cost of lost productivity for those who cannot work from home.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal masks a 'one-size-fits-all' approach under the guise of climate awareness, ignoring the structural inequalities that dictate mobility choices. It assumes that 'walking' is a free choice for all, failing to recognize that for the working poor, skipping the bus is not an option but a survival constraint. The projected long-term impacts (recycling, solar law, electric buses) are presented as inevitable outcomes of a single day of walking, a normative leap that obscures the need for systemic policy intervention rather than voluntary behavioral nudges.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal implicitly excludes low-income workers who rely on public transport for survival and cannot work from home, framing their inability to walk as a lack of 'altruistic spirit' rather than a structural constraint.

False Causality: The claim that a one-day walking event will directly cause a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of solar laws is a logical non-sequitur that hides the complexity of policy change.
Safety & Equity: The proposal encourages walking in a high-density city without addressing the lack of safe pedestrian infrastructure, potentially exposing vulnerable populations to traffic violence.

Data Privacy & Power: The plan to hand over 'big data' on citizen movement to Google and sponsors raises significant concerns about surveillance capitalism and who ultimately owns the insights generated from public behavior.

Cost Distribution: The costs of security, health support, and media are listed, but the hidden costs (time lost, safety risks, lost wages for those who cannot WFH) are borne entirely by the participants, not the sponsors or government.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a fragile chain of assumptions: that a single-day event will trigger government mandates for home office, that sponsors will fund a complex app, and that the government will re-route public transport based on one day's data. The logistical requirement for 'security forces' and 'ambulances' for a walking event suggests a misunderstanding of the activity's risk profile, while the dependency on Google to build a custom app is a critical single point of failure that makes execution highly improbable without massive, unsecured capital.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a well-established, decades-old practice in urban planning and public health. The proposal adds a smartphone tracking layer, but this is a standard gamification tactic used in countless existing apps (e.g., Strava, Google Fit). There is no systemic innovation here; it is a re-packaging of existing behavioral nudges without addressing the structural barriers to sustainable mobility.

impact.rationale: The proposal's long-term impact claims are mathematically and logically unsound. It projects that a one-day event will lead to a 10% increase in recycling, the approval of solar laws, and the implementation of electric buses. These are structural policy shifts driven by legislation and capital investment, not by a single day of walking. The calculation that a 1-day event will result in 39 million tons of CO2e savings globally is a gross extrapolation error that ignores the difference between a temporary behavioral anomaly and a permanent systemic shift. This is 'shift' (temporary reduction) masquerading as 'resilience' (permanent change).

presentation.rationale: The presentation is cluttered with broken image references (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png'), inconsistent formatting, and a confusing narrative that jumps between a 'practice' and a 'project.' The impact section presents a 'black box' of calculations where short-term event data is conflated with long-term policy outcomes without a causal mechanism, making the argument difficult to follow and scientifically weak.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term sustainability lens, this proposal is a classic example of 'awareness theater' that mistakes a temporary reduction in emissions for systemic resilience. It fails to address the structural inertia of urban transport systems and relies on magical thinking regarding policy adoption. The projected long-term impacts are not derived from the event's mechanics but are simply wish-list items for the city, disconnected from the actual intervention.
key_concerns: The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral nudge with multi-year structural policy changes (e.g., passing solar laws, electrifying bus fleets) without explaining the causal mechanism.
The 'long-term impact' calculations are mathematically invalid, assuming a single day's participation scales linearly to global annual savings without accounting for habit decay or rebound effects.
There is no plan for what happens after the event; if the 'home office' or 'walking' habits are not institutionalized by employers and city planners, the impact reverts to zero immediately.

The proposal assumes government and corporate cooperation (re-routing buses, mandating home office) will happen automatically due to 'altruism,' ignoring the political economy and cost-benefit analysis required for such shifts.
The reliance on a custom Google app and 'big data' for a one-day event introduces high technical risk and privacy concerns for a low-yield outcome.

A1.text: The consensus across the pool (A2-A6) validates my initial assessment of the proposal's scientific and systemic fragility. Specifically, A4 and A5 introduce critical dimensions I under-weighted: the 'equity gap' (excluding low-income workers who cannot WFH or walk safely) and the 'safety infrastructure' deficit in Buenos Aires. These are not just social concerns; they are system-level failure modes that render the '5% participation' assumption empirically unsound for a heterogeneous population. A6's distinction between 'shift' (temporary reduction) and 'resilience' (permanent change) sharply clarifies the causal fallacy in the long-term impact model. The proposal treats a one-day behavioral nudge as a lever for capital-intensive infrastructure (electric buses, solar laws) without a defined transmission mechanism. This confirms the 'Impact' score of 2 is actually an upper bound; the causal link is effectively non-existent. I maintain my scores, as the pool reinforces that the proposal rests on 'magical thinking' regarding policy causality rather than evidence-based system dynamics.

A2.text: The consensus across the pool (A1, A3, A4, A5, A6) validates my initial assessment: the proposal suffers from a fundamental 'governance gap' where a one-day behavioral nudge is erroneously conflated with long-term structural policy reform. However, A5's focus on 'distributional injustice' and A4's emphasis on 'accessibility exclusion' sharpen the critique significantly. The proposal's assumption that 'skipping the bus' is a universal choice ignores the reality that for low-income workers in Buenos Aires, public transport is a necessity, not a luxury. This makes the 'voluntary' nature of the event a mechanism for excluding the very populations most vulnerable to climate impacts. Furthermore, A1 and A6 correctly identify the 'causal fallacy' regarding the electric bus and solar law projections; these are capital-intensive, multi-year legislative processes that cannot be triggered by a single day of walking data. The proposal's reliance on 'Google' to build a custom app and the government to re-route transit without a formal regulatory framework or budget is not just optimistic; it is institutionally incoherent. While the presentation (score 3) is structurally complete, the internal logic is fractured. I maintain my scores, as the pool confirms that the proposal fails on feasibility (no regulatory lever), novelty (repackaged awareness), and impact (mathematically unsound extrapolation). The only adjustment is a heightened awareness of the equity risks highlighted by A5, which further depresses the feasibility of a 'one-size-fits-all' approach.

A3.text:
The consensus across the pool (A1, A2, A4, A5, A6) is overwhelming: the proposal's causal logic is fundamentally broken. I initially flagged the operational fragility (A3), but A4 and A5 add a critical dimension I underweighted: the *equity and safety* implications of mandating a 'walk to work' day in a city with significant infrastructure gaps and a large informal workforce. A4's point on 'Accessibility Exclusion' and A5's on 'False Causality' sharpen the critique that this isn't just a logistical failure, but a potential policy hazard that could alienate the very populations it claims to help.

However, I must contest the uniformity of the 'Impact' score of 1 or 2. While the *long-term* extrapolations (39M tons) are indeed scientifically absurd and constitute 'awareness theater' (A6), the *short-term* metric of 66 tCO2e is a real, albeit small, physical reduction. The proposal's fatal flaw is not the event itself, but the *attribution* of systemic policy changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) to a single day of walking. A1 and A2 correctly identify this as a 'causal fallacy.'

My revised judgment: The proposal is not a 'climate solution' but a 'marketing stunt' with high reputational risk. The 'Big Data' value proposition is nullified by the lack of a privacy framework (A2, A5) and the low probability of Google's free involvement. The 'Business Model' is non-existent; it relies entirely on 'altruism' and unsecured government mandates. I am lowering my Feasibility score to 1 (from 2) because the dependency on a 'perfect storm' of free corporate labor and immediate government policy shifts makes execution probability near zero. The Impact score remains 2, but with a caveat: the short-term impact is real but negligible, while the long-term claims are hallucinations.

A4.text: The consensus from A1, A2, A3, A5, and A6 on the 'causal fallacy' (linking a one-day walk to solar laws and electric bus fleets) is correct and devastating to the proposal's credibility. However, my initial critique focused heavily on the *logistical* impossibility of the event. Reading A5's point on 'Exclusion Risk' and A1's note on the 'rebound effect' sharpens my view: this isn't just a failed logistics plan; it is a socially regressive one. The proposal explicitly tells people to 'skip the bus' and 'turn to their own bodies,' which, in a city like Buenos Aires with significant inequality, effectively tells low-income workers, the elderly, and the disabled that their mobility is a moral failing if they cannot walk 15 blocks. The 'Big Data' extraction by Google and sponsors (A5) further exploits the very populations the event claims to help, turning their movement into a commodity without addressing the safety gaps (A1, A3) that make walking dangerous for them. The proposal's 'altruistic spirit' is a veneer for a top-down exercise that ignores the structural constraints of the poor. I am revising my Impact score down to 1, as the potential for social harm and cynicism outweighs the negligible, temporary emission savings. The 'impact' is not just low; it is potentially negative for social cohesion and trust in climate initiatives.

A5.text: The consensus from A1, A2, A3, A4, and A6 on the proposal's technical and causal flaws is overwhelming and correct; however, my initial assessment of the *normative* stakes requires sharpening against the group's focus on 'feasibility' and 'math.' While A4 rightly identifies the exclusion of the disabled and the 'home office' privilege, the group collectively underestimates the *coercive* nature of the proposal's 'voluntary' framing. The proposal explicitly asks the government to 'arrange' transport modifications and 'ask' companies to mandate home office, effectively outsourcing structural policy failures to a one-day behavioral nudge. This is not just a feasibility error; it is a justice failure. It shifts the burden of climate action onto individuals who lack the agency to change their commute (the poor, the disabled, the service worker) while absolving the state and corporations of their duty to provide safe infrastructure and flexible labor policies. The 'Big Data' extraction by Google and sponsors, framed as a benefit, is actually a surveillance mechanism that monetizes the movement of the very people the proposal claims to help, without their informed consent or a share in the value generated. I maintain my scores, but I emphasize that the 'Impact' score of 2 is generous: the proposal's *negative* distributional consequences (excluding the vulnerable, privatizing public data) may outweigh its negligible positive emissions savings. The 'novelty' is not just low; it is regressive, repackaging old awareness tactics that ignore the structural roots of inequality.

A6.text:
The consensus from A1-A5 is robust: this proposal is a classic case of 'awareness theater' mistaking a temporary behavioral nudge for systemic resilience. I particularly align with A4 and A5 on the critical equity blind spots—framing 'skipping the bus' as a choice ignores the structural reality that for the working poor, public transport is a survival necessity, not a luxury to be forgone. A2 and A3 correctly dismantle the 'magic wand' governance assumptions, exposing the logical fallacy that a single day of walking can force the passage of solar laws or the electrification of bus fleets.

However, I must sharpen the disagreement on the *nature* of the failure. While A1-A5 focus on the mathematical unsoundness of the impact claims, my primary concern is the *temporal disconnect*. The proposal does not just overestimate impact; it actively misrepresents the mechanism of change. It treats a one-day event as a catalyst for multi-year capital investment cycles (electric buses, solar laws) without a single intermediate step (e.g., policy advocacy, infrastructure budgeting, pilot programs). This is not merely an 'optimistic forecast'; it is a category error in systems thinking. The proposal suggests that 'awareness' alone can bypass the inertia of political economy and capital markets.

Furthermore, the 'Big Data' value proposition is a red herring. As A5 noted, handing citizen movement data to Google and sponsors for a one-day event creates a surveillance risk that vastly outweighs the negligible, transient emission savings. The 'rebound effect' (A4) is also understated; if participants return to car usage the next day, the net impact is zero, yet the proposal claims permanent habit formation without evidence of institutionalization (e.g., mandatory WFH policies, permanent infrastructure changes).

My initial scores (Feasibility: 2, Novelty: 2, Impact: 1, Presentation: 2) remain valid. The 'Impact' score of 1 is justified because the proposal's long-term claims are not just optimistic; they are causally unconnected to the intervention. The event cannot 'cause' the approval of a solar law. The 'Feasibility' score of 2 reflects the near-zero probability of the required government/corporate alignment without a pre-existing policy framework. No revision is needed; the pool confirms that this is a high-risk, low-yield intervention that shifts the burden of systemic change onto individual altruism while ignoring the structural barriers that make walking or biking impossible for many.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'perfect storm' of unverified assumptions: 5% of the city's population voluntarily walking/biking to work on a single day, simultaneous government mandate for home-office, and the immediate development of a custom tracking app. The causal chain assumes that a one-day behavioral nudge will trigger complex systemic changes (e.g., 2% of bus lines converting to electric, 1% city demand covered by solar) without intermediate policy or infrastructure steps. The dependency on 'Google' to develop the app and 'Government' to re-route transport creates a high-risk implementation bottleneck.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada. The only novel element is the proposed aggregation of 'big data' via a smartphone app, but the technical approach (using existing footprint algorithms) is standard. The novelty is low because the intervention is a repetition of known awareness campaigns rather than a new mechanism for emission reduction.

impact.rationale: The quantitative claims are scientifically unsound due to massive extrapolation errors. The proposal calculates long-term impacts (e.g., 35,700 tonnes of CO2e saved from electric buses) as a direct result of a one-day walking event, ignoring the multi-year capital investment and policy cycles required to deploy electric buses. The extrapolation from Buenos Aires to the world (scaling 5% participation to 1 billion people) assumes linear scalability of behavioral change, which contradicts empirical evidence on diffusion of innovations. The short-term impact (66 tCO2e) is negligible on a global scale, and the long-term attribution is causally unproven.

presentation.rationale: The proposal lacks scientific rigor in its data presentation. It mixes short-term behavioral changes with long-term infrastructure outcomes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) without distinguishing between correlation and causation. The mathematical derivations contain unit inconsistencies and circular logic (e.g., assuming the event causes the approval of solar laws). The text relies heavily on 'we expect' and 'we forecast' without citing empirical baselines or sensitivity analyses for the variables used.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal conflates a one-day awareness event with long-term structural decarbonization. The causal mechanisms linking a single day of walking to the deployment of electric bus fleets and solar legislation are non-existent. The quantitative impact model is fundamentally flawed, treating complex, capital-intensive infrastructure transitions as immediate, linear consequences of a behavioral nudge. While the intent is positive, the scientific plausibility of the claimed outcomes is extremely low.
key_concerns: Causal Fallacy: The proposal attributes long-term infrastructure outcomes (electric bus deployment, solar law approval) to a single-day awareness event without explaining the intermediate policy or investment mechanisms.
Extrapolation Error: Scaling a 5% participation rate in Buenos Aires to a global population assumes uniform behavioral adoption, ignoring cultural, infrastructural, and economic heterogeneity.

Implementation Risk: The plan depends on the immediate, voluntary cooperation of Google for app development and the government for transport re-routing, which are high-friction, low-probability events.
Data Integrity: The 'big data' value proposition is speculative; without a validated baseline of individual commuting habits, the calculated GHG savings are likely inaccurate.

System Interdependencies: The proposal ignores the 'rebound effect' and the fact that public transport (buses/subways) is often more efficient per capita than walking for long distances; skipping them entirely may not yield the net savings claimed if the alternative is inefficient private transport or if the walking distance is negligible compared to total commute.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' governance assumption: that a single-day awareness event can force structural changes in public transport efficiency, pass distributed solar laws, and mandate corporate home-office policies. The text explicitly states the government 'will have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes' and 'ask for their cooperation by allowing employees to work from home,' ignoring the legal, labor, and operational inertia required to enact such changes. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and the assumption that 5% of the population will voluntarily track data without a robust privacy framework or data governance strategy presents significant regulatory and logistical hurdles.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day) with no novel governance mechanisms or policy innovations proposed. The proposal attempts to rebrand existing initiatives (like Buenos Aires' 'Ecobici') as a new project without introducing a new institutional model or regulatory lever. The 'novelty' is purely rhetorical, not structural.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically unsound and politically naive. The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral shift with long-term structural policy outcomes (e.g., claiming a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of a solar law as direct results of a walking event). The extrapolation of 39 million tCO2e/year globally based on a single-day event in Buenos Aires is a gross overestimation that ignores the difference between a temporary anomaly and sustained behavioral change. The governance capacity to enforce the 'long-term' impacts listed (e.g., 2% electric bus implementation) is not demonstrated.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Canvas, Impact, Costs), but the internal logic is fractured. It oscillates between a simple awareness campaign and a complex policy reform agenda without explaining the causal link between the two. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and the lack of a clear, step-by-step regulatory pathway for the government's role weaken the professional presentation. The tone is overly optimistic and lacks the rigor expected for a policy proposal.

A2.perspective_summary: From a governance perspective, this proposal is a classic case of 'solutionism' where a simple behavioral nudge is expected to solve complex systemic policy failures. It fails to distinguish between an event (which can be organized) and a policy regime (which requires legislation, budget, and enforcement). The proposal assumes the government will act as a passive enabler rather than a complex stakeholder with competing priorities, rendering the long-term impact claims unrealistic without a detailed implementation roadmap.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes a causal link between a one-day walking event and the passage of complex legislation (Distributed Solar Law) and operational changes in public transport, which is institutionally incoherent.

There is no data governance strategy for the proposed smartphone app; collecting location and movement data from 5% of the population raises significant privacy and security concerns that are not addressed.

The 'long-term' impact metrics (e.g., 10% recycling increase) are presented as direct outcomes of the event without a mechanism for enforcement or sustained behavioral change, making the impact claims speculative and likely inflated.

The proposal relies on 'voluntary' corporate cooperation for home-office days, ignoring labor laws, union agreements, and operational constraints that would likely block such mandates without formal policy intervention.
The extrapolation of local, one-day data to global, annual emission savings is methodologically flawed and undermines the credibility of the impact assessment.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big government' coordination model that is operationally fragile: it assumes the government will simultaneously mandate home-office days, re-route public transport, and authorize a city-wide event without a clear implementation roadmap. The technical requirement for a custom app to track routes and calculate GHG savings in real-time, while expecting Google to build it for free, is a critical resource gap. Furthermore, the assumption that 5% of the population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily walk to work on a single day ignores the logistical reality of Buenos Aires' density and the lack of infrastructure for such a mass shift in a single day.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a well-established global practice (referenced correctly in the text as existing in the US/Canada). The proposal adds a 'gamification' layer via an app and data tracking, but this is a standard feature in existing fitness and mobility apps (e.g., Strava, Citymapper). There is no novel mechanism for behavior change or carbon accounting; it is a repackaging of existing awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are mathematically unsound and operationally unrealistic. The proposal extrapolates a one-day event's short-term savings (66 tCO2e) into long-term structural changes (e.g., 35,700 tonnes saved via electric buses) based on a 5% participation rate. This conflates a one-off awareness event with systemic infrastructure policy. The claim that a single day of walking will lead to a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws is a logical leap with no causal mechanism provided. The 'Big Data' value is speculative without a guaranteed user base or data privacy framework.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard sections (Business Model Canvas, Impact calculations), but the internal logic is inconsistent. It oscillates between a 'practice' and a 'project' without defining the operational scope clearly. The reliance on external entities (Google, Government, MIT) to execute critical path items (app development, policy changes) without a defined governance structure or budget makes the narrative optimistic rather than pragmatic. The inclusion of image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without the actual content in the text reduces clarity.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' masquerading as a scalable climate solution. It fails to account for the massive coordination costs, the lack of a sustainable business model for the app, and the logical fallacy of deriving long-term policy outcomes from a single-day behavioral nudge. The reliance on 'altruistic spirit' and free government/tech support is a high-risk assumption that ignores the resource intensity of city-wide logistics.
key_concerns: Operational Feasibility: How does the proposal secure government authorization to re-route public transport and mandate home-office days without a legislative or executive order? This is a political, not just logistical, hurdle.

Resource Gap: The proposal assumes Google will develop a custom tracking app for free. What is the fallback if this does not happen? Who funds the security, medical, and media costs listed in the 'Costs' section?

Causal Logic: The jump from a one-day walking event to a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of solar laws is unsupported. What is the specific mechanism that translates 'walking' into 'policy change'?
Data Privacy & Liability: Collecting location data for 145,000+ users on a single day raises significant privacy and liability issues (e.g., accidents, data breaches) that are not addressed.

Scalability: The model relies on high-density urban centers (Buenos Aires). How does this scale to lower-density areas or countries with different public transport infrastructures (e.g., US suburbs) where walking is not a viable commute option?

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will instantly re-route public transport and force companies to allow home office for a single-day event without a pilot phase. The requirement for a custom Google-developed app to track specific GHG savings in real-time is technically and bureaucratically unrealistic for a one-off awareness day. Furthermore, the logistics of providing health support and security for a city-wide walking event without a clear route management plan present significant safety risks.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Bike to Work' or 'Walk to Work' days—is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada and the local 'Ecobici' system. The only slight variation is the attempt to gamify it with a specific GHG-tracking app, but the underlying behavioral mechanism is identical to decades of existing campaigns.

impact.rationale: The impact calculations are dangerously inflated and disconnected from ground reality. Extrapolating a single day's savings to '39 million tCO2e/year' globally is mathematically unsound and ignores the rebound effect (people driving more the next day). The proposal assumes a 5% participation rate will trigger systemic changes like a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws, which are policy outcomes, not direct results of a one-day walk. The actual short-term impact is negligible compared to the massive long-term claims.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with standard business canvas elements and includes specific (albeit flawed) calculations, which shows effort. However, the presentation suffers from a lack of critical self-reflection regarding the 'unintended effects' on vulnerable populations. It presents an idealized, frictionless version of the city that ignores the reality of Buenos Aires' infrastructure gaps, safety concerns for pedestrians, and the accessibility needs of those who cannot walk or bike.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down exercise in 'feel-good' metrics that ignores the daily friction of living in a dense, unequal city. It assumes everyone has the physical ability to walk 15 blocks, the safety to do so, and the privilege to work from home. The massive extrapolation of impact suggests a disconnect between the planners and the actual lived experience of the community, where a single day of walking does not magically fix systemic transport or policy failures.
key_concerns: Accessibility Exclusion: The proposal assumes universal physical ability to walk or bike, ignoring the elderly, disabled, and those with chronic conditions who rely on public transport or cars for mobility.

Safety and Infrastructure: Buenos Aires has significant gaps in safe pedestrian and cycling infrastructure. Encouraging mass walking/biking without addressing traffic safety or sidewalk conditions could lead to accidents, particularly for children and the elderly.

The 'Home Office' Privilege: The suggestion that people far from work can simply 'do home office' ignores the reality of the gig economy, service sector workers, and low-income employees who cannot work remotely.

Data Privacy and Surveillance: The proposal relies on a smartphone app tracking location and movement data for 'Big Data' analysis by sponsors and Google, raising serious privacy concerns for participants without clear consent frameworks.

Unrealistic Policy Leverage: The assumption that a one-day event will force the government to re-route buses or pass solar laws is a fundamental misunderstanding of how policy change works; this risks cynicism if the promised systemic changes do not materialize.

Rebound Effect: There is no plan to ensure that the 'saved' emissions aren't simply offset by increased driving on other days or by the carbon footprint of the event's logistics (media, prizes, app development).

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'magic wand' assumption that the government will mandate home office and restructure bus routes based on a single day's event data. The text admits the government must 'arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes,' a logistical impossibility for a one-day awareness event. Furthermore, the reliance on a Google-developed app and immediate government authorization ignores the bureaucratic inertia and data privacy hurdles inherent in such public-private data exchanges.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Bike to Work' or 'Walk to Work' days—is a well-established global practice, not an innovation. The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US and Canada and Buenos Aires' own 'Ecobici' system. The only novel element is the aggregation of data for 'big data' insights, but the methodology (tracking steps to calculate carbon savings) is standard and lacks a unique mechanism for behavioral change beyond existing campaigns.

impact.rationale: The proposal hides a severe distributional injustice and a logical fallacy in its impact claims. It assumes a 5% participation rate will trigger a 10% increase in recycling and a 2% adoption of electric buses over five years, a causal link that is entirely speculative. More critically, the proposal excludes low-income populations who cannot afford to 'skip the bus' or work from home; for them, public transport is a necessity, not a choice. The 'savings' calculated ignore the fact that the poor often live in disconnected areas where walking is unsafe or impossible, effectively penalizing the most vulnerable for not participating.

presentation.rationale: The presentation is structurally coherent but normatively naive. It uses a Business Model Canvas and specific calculations to lend scientific weight to what are essentially aspirational guesses. The text fails to address the 'who bears the cost' question, presenting the event as universally beneficial while ignoring the safety risks of walking in high-density, high-traffic areas without infrastructure upgrades, and the economic cost of lost productivity for those who cannot work from home.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal masks a 'one-size-fits-all' approach under the guise of climate awareness, ignoring the structural inequalities that dictate mobility choices. It assumes that 'walking' is a free choice for all, failing to recognize that for the working poor, skipping the bus is not an option but a survival constraint. The projected long-term impacts (recycling, solar law, electric buses) are presented as inevitable outcomes of a single day of walking, a normative leap that obscures the need for systemic policy intervention rather than voluntary behavioral nudges.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal implicitly excludes low-income workers who rely on public transport for survival and cannot work from home, framing their inability to walk as a lack of 'altruistic spirit' rather than a structural constraint.

False Causality: The claim that a one-day walking event will directly cause a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of solar laws is a logical non-sequitur that hides the complexity of policy change.
Safety & Equity: The proposal encourages walking in a high-density city without addressing the lack of safe pedestrian infrastructure, potentially exposing vulnerable populations to traffic violence.

Data Privacy & Power: The plan to hand over 'big data' on citizen movement to Google and sponsors raises significant concerns about surveillance capitalism and who ultimately owns the insights generated from public behavior.

Cost Distribution: The costs of security, health support, and media are listed, but the hidden costs (time lost, safety risks, lost wages for those who cannot WFH) are borne entirely by the participants, not the sponsors or government.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a fragile chain of assumptions: that a single-day event will trigger government mandates for home office, that sponsors will fund a complex app, and that the government will re-route public transport based on one day's data. The logistical requirement for 'security forces' and 'ambulances' for a walking event suggests a misunderstanding of the activity's risk profile, while the dependency on Google to build a custom app is a critical single point of failure that makes execution highly improbable without massive, unsecured capital.

novelty.rationale: The core concept—'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days—is a well-established, decades-old practice in urban planning and public health. The proposal adds a smartphone tracking layer, but this is a standard gamification tactic used in countless existing apps (e.g., Strava, Google Fit). There is no systemic innovation here; it is a re-packaging of existing behavioral nudges without addressing the structural barriers to sustainable mobility.

impact.rationale: The proposal's long-term impact claims are mathematically and logically unsound. It projects that a one-day event will lead to a 10% increase in recycling, the approval of solar laws, and the implementation of electric buses. These are structural policy shifts driven by legislation and capital investment, not by a single day of walking. The calculation that a 1-day event will result in 39 million tons of CO2e savings globally is a gross extrapolation error that ignores the difference between a temporary behavioral anomaly and a permanent systemic shift. This is 'shift' (temporary reduction) masquerading as 'resilience' (permanent change).

presentation.rationale: The presentation is cluttered with broken image references (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png'), inconsistent formatting, and a confusing narrative that jumps between a 'practice' and a 'project.' The impact section presents a 'black box' of calculations where short-term event data is conflated with long-term policy outcomes without a causal mechanism, making the argument difficult to follow and scientifically weak.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term sustainability lens, this proposal is a classic example of 'awareness theater' that mistakes a temporary reduction in emissions for systemic resilience. It fails to address the structural inertia of urban transport systems and relies on magical thinking regarding policy adoption. The projected long-term impacts are not derived from the event's mechanics but are simply wish-list items for the city, disconnected from the actual intervention.
key_concerns: The proposal conflates a one-day behavioral nudge with multi-year structural policy changes (e.g., passing solar laws, electrifying bus fleets) without explaining the causal mechanism.
The 'long-term impact' calculations are mathematically invalid, assuming a single day's participation scales linearly to global annual savings without accounting for habit decay or rebound effects.
There is no plan for what happens after the event; if the 'home office' or 'walking' habits are not institutionalized by employers and city planners, the impact reverts to zero immediately.

The proposal assumes government and corporate cooperation (re-routing buses, mandating home office) will happen automatically due to 'altruism,' ignoring the political economy and cost-benefit analysis required for such shifts.
The reliance on a custom Google app and 'big data' for a one-day event introduces high technical risk and privacy concerns for a low-yield outcome.

clusters.label: The Causal Fallacy: Awareness vs. Structural Change
clusters.summary: A unanimous consensus that the proposal commits a fundamental category error by treating a one-day behavioral nudge (walking/biking) as a direct causal lever for complex, capital-intensive structural outcomes (passing solar laws, electrifying bus fleets, increasing recycling rates). Experts argue there is no defined transmission mechanism linking a single day of 'awareness' to multi-year legislative or infrastructure cycles, rendering the long-term impact claims scientifically unsound and logically incoherent.

clusters.label: Equity Blind Spots and Distributional Injustice
clusters.summary: A critical cluster focusing on the proposal's exclusionary nature. Experts highlight that the 'voluntary' framing ignores structural inequalities: low-income workers, the disabled, and the elderly cannot 'skip the bus' or work from home. The proposal effectively penalizes the most vulnerable for lacking the privilege to walk, while the 'Big Data' extraction by Google and sponsors is framed as surveillance capitalism that monetizes the movement of the poor without addressing their safety or agency.

clusters.label: Governance Fragility and Implementation Risks
clusters.summary: Experts identify a 'magic wand' governance assumption where the proposal relies on the immediate, free cooperation of Google for app development and the government for re-routing transport and mandating home-office days. The consensus is that these dependencies are high-risk, low-probability events that ignore bureaucratic inertia, legal constraints, and the lack of a sustainable business model or budget, making the project operationally unfeasible.

clusters.label: Mathematical Extrapolation and Data Integrity
clusters.summary: A technical critique of the proposal's quantitative model. Experts condemn the extrapolation of a single day's short-term savings (66 tCO2e) to global annual figures (39 million tCO2e) and the assumption of linear scalability. The 'Big Data' value proposition is deemed speculative due to the lack of a privacy framework, baseline data, and the high probability of the 'rebound effect' negating any temporary emission reductions.

open_questions: If the causal link between a one-day event and long-term policy change (solar laws, electric buses) is non-existent, what specific intermediate mechanisms (e.g., policy advocacy, pilot programs) must be inserted to make the impact claims plausible?

How can the proposal be redesigned to address the 'equity gap' where the 'voluntary' nature of the event effectively excludes low-income and disabled populations, turning a climate initiative into a tool of social exclusion?

Given the consensus that the 'Big Data' extraction by Google poses a surveillance risk with negligible return, what alternative data governance model could ensure privacy and community ownership of the insights generated?

Is there any scenario where a 'one-day awareness event' can legitimately claim credit for systemic infrastructure changes, or must the proposal be reclassified entirely as a 'marketing stunt' with no substantive climate impact?

feasibility.rationale: The proposal is operationally unfeasible due to a 'magic wand' governance assumption: it relies on the immediate, free cooperation of Google for app development and the government to re-route transport and mandate home-office days without a legislative or budgetary roadmap. The logistical requirement to coordinate security, health support, and mass behavioral shifts for a single day, while assuming a 5% voluntary participation rate in a dense city without infrastructure support, presents a critical implementation bottleneck that renders the project impossible to execute as described.

novelty.rationale: The core intervention—a 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' day—is a well-established global practice with no novel mechanisms for behavior change or carbon accounting. The proposal explicitly cites existing programs in the US, Canada, and Buenos Aires' own 'Ecobici' system. The only attempted innovation is the gamification of data tracking via a smartphone app, which utilizes standard algorithms and offers no unique structural advantage over existing fitness or mobility platforms.

impact.rationale: The quantitative impact claims are scientifically unsound and logically incoherent. The proposal commits a fundamental causal fallacy by attributing long-term, capital-intensive structural outcomes (e.g., 10% recycling increase, approval of solar laws, 2% electric bus fleet conversion) to a single-day behavioral nudge. Furthermore, the extrapolation of a one-day local saving (66 tCO2e) to a global annual figure (39 million tCO2e) ignores habit decay, rebound effects, and the non-linear nature of systemic change, rendering the impact assessment a gross overestimation.

presentation.rationale: While the proposal utilizes standard structural elements like a Business Model Canvas, the internal logic is fractured, oscillating between a simple awareness event and a complex policy reform agenda without defining the causal links. The presentation relies heavily on image placeholders, lacks a clear regulatory pathway, and presents mathematical derivations that conflate correlation with causation. The tone is overly optimistic, lacking the rigor and self-reflection required for a policy proposal of this scope.

final.synthesis: The expert pool reached a unanimous consensus on the proposal's fundamental flaws, specifically the 'Causal Fallacy' where a one-day event is incorrectly linked to multi-year structural policy changes. There was no substantive disagreement to resolve; rather, the synthesis involved reinforcing the collective critique that the proposal confuses 'awareness theater' with 'systemic resilience.' The primary tension lay between the proposal's aspirational tone and the experts' rigorous demand for evidence-based mechanisms. This was resolved by rejecting the proposal's long-term impact claims entirely, as no intermediate mechanisms (e.g., advocacy, pilot programs) were provided to bridge the gap between a walking event and the passage of solar laws. The 'Equity Blind Spots' identified by A4 and A5 were integrated as a critical feasibility constraint, noting that the 'voluntary' nature of the event inherently excludes vulnerable populations, further lowering the feasibility score. The 'Governance Fragility' was accepted as a definitive blocker, as the reliance on unsecured government mandates and free corporate development (Google) is not a viable strategy. Consequently, the final judgment reflects a near-total rejection of the proposal's current form across all dimensions.
final.uncertainty: The only remaining uncertainty is whether the proposal could be salvaged by completely decoupling the 'event' from the 'policy outcomes.' If the proposal were reclassified strictly as a short-term awareness campaign with no claims of long-term legislative impact, the 'Impact' score might rise slightly, though the 'Feasibility' would remain low due to the unsecured dependencies on Google and the government. However, as currently written, the causal links are so broken that no amount of minor adjustment can validate the core thesis. The question of whether a 'one-day event' can ever legitimately claim credit for systemic change remains a theoretical open question, but for this specific proposal, the answer is definitively no.
